speaker: interviewee
text: So I report to this guard room. And I'm sitting now in the guardroom with this guard saying, Mengele is due any moment. And I sit there, like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an interminable age. And of course nobody arrives. So eventually I say to myself, I've got to go. You know, I've, I've, I've got to say I've got to go back to the camp. So I say to this guard that I'd like to report for appell, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, back to my barrack, because they won't know where I am. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he laughs and he says, no, I will be counting you here. I know where you are. [LAUGHS] You stay put. You wait for Mengele.

speaker: interviewee
text: But eventually, I keep-- I asked him again. He lets me go. I get into the camp and there's appell. And it's getting really dark. And suddenly I see about five or six officers downhill. Auschwitz is completely flat, so you can't see anything, you see. You haven't got any view of the surroundings because it's flat, at least Birkenau is. Auschwitz I was on the hill. But Birkenau was completely flat in the Valley of the Vistula.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could see this from Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Whilst you were in Auschwitz, in Birkenau, you couldn't orientate yourself because you were on completely flat ground. The only thing that you could look at is the sky and these interminable fences. Gross-Rosen was different because it was hilly. It was on a hill, so you could see down the hill to the guard house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we're standing at appell, and these officers are arriving at the bottom wire. And I can see amongst then Mengele, and he sees me being counted, and he motions to me to come to the wire. He doesn't come into the camp. He wants me to come to the wire. And because it was getting dark and we were being dismissed, I sort of made as if I didn't see him, and I disappeared, because I didn't want any favors from him at this juncture. Who knows what he wanted to-- what he would've thought of me asking for a job in Gross-Rosen when we were destined to leave anyway? I mean, I didn't know that we were leaving, but we did leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we were put into cattle trucks again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and it was now really cold, and people died quite, quite badly. So we were throwing them out of the truck. So the truck got emptier. There was more and more room as the journey proceeded. And the Germans would put us in a siding, wait all day, and then we would go through the night. This was all done, you know, so that the population shouldn't see us. And we didn't know where we were. We kept-- here and there, you would see someone, so we would ask where we are. And they would tell us a name, and you wouldn't know where it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What names do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. And I don't-- I didn't know the geography of Germany. I knew some of the names of towns, but I didn't know where the towns were. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the other prisoners were throwing these bodies out that died. It's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't even tell you about the rollwagen. Dead bodies are, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are very difficult to handle, because when you lift it in one place, it falls down in another place. And with this rollwagen in the mud, the bodies would be loaded, and then they would slither through these, these fence-like leather-- the leather-type sides. And the men had to pick them up again and load them again. And now it was the same sort of thing, because somebody would die, he would be stripped naked, and he'd be thrown. And people would grab legs and hands, and two or three swings and then, up, and he'd fly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the body would fly like a pole vaulter. I was telling you about my brothers being tremendous sportsmen, particularly Shragga. He used to jump pole. I mean, they were Olympic-- all my brothers were Olympic-type sportsmen. Henry, for example, got third prize in a skiing competition in Czechoslovakia. And Shragga could jump 16 feet or something, pole vaulting. And the record was half a meter more or something like that. And I remember this curve, you know, that a pole vaulter-- and I thought of Shragga jumping like this, that these bodies were vaulting exactly like a pole vaulter, this backward roll. And we got to Buchenwald. And the Buchenwald commandant refused to take us because Buchenwald was full. So the journey continued somewhere else. And I don't know how long it took, but it, it must have taken two weeks, probably more. I don't remember how we were fed. Most of the time we were not fed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we were terribly hungry, particularly thirsty. People would jump out of the train when it was in the sidings and risk their life to gather up the snow, you know, to, to drink, to have something on the lips. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: One of those mornings we stop, and a farmer is going over one of these humpbacked bridges over the railway. And he, he asks us who we are, you see? And we said, we are Jews from Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Auschwitz is in Silesia. Where are we? So we were still in Silesia. And he named a place. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So they asked him for some food. And he said, I've got nothing on me. Anyway, I wouldn't give it to Jews. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, and he said something, you're all guilty. That's why we've put you into camps. What did he mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Guilty of what? What did he mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what he meant. Catholic guilt. It wasn't a discussion. It was sort of, he was on the bridge, we were in the wagon underneath.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put me in front and said, look. There's a kid. Is he guilty of anything? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he says, well, he must have done something if German justice put him into prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whilst we were talking, two men jumped out and they jumped him, dragged him back into the train, took his clothes and his papers that he had on him, and they disappeared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was dressed in these rags amongst us. And he said, wait till I tell them, you know, at the next station. And we arrive at the station, and he goes to a German guard, and he talks to him in German. And he says, you know, I'm a German. I'm not with these Jews. And the German looks at him and takes his rifle butt and gives him one with the rifle butt, the way he would do to a Jewish prisoner. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this man was completely broken, German or no German, he didn't know. You know, it was like a different dimension. He was thrown back into this thing amongst us, and he died. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- I shouldn't think from the rifle butt, but he died. He was so broken. You know, how can this be happening to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we got to Mauthausen and were put up in a tent camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about February, early February. Luckily the weather had broken, because the tent camp was-- you know, this tent camp factory in, in, in Theresienstadt, which they built for making bindings, it was roofs made out of tenting and sides halfway down, and the bottom of the tent was exposed to the elements. So you were, like, sleeping in the open, but you had a roof over your head. So rain was all right, but cold wasn't. And we got into a camp with these Hungarian Jews whom they didn't manage to gas. And they still had their packages with them and various things. They still had their rucksacks, you see. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I began to see some of the boys, some of the Birkenau boys, like Yehuda Backon. And this Polish boy was with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said again, look, go to the guard. Tell him you want to be a laufer. Go out to get some food somewhere. We got to help ourselves. So I took my band off, and I said, look. You be the laufer, because I didn't want to any more. I thought it's too dangerous. And the experience with Mengele sort of unnerved me. So now he became the laufer. And he started giving me soup. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And our positions had changed, you see, because I was the one in Auschwitz who was-- I played boss. Now I never played like that. I learned not to treat people like that. You know, this was like, I don't know who this boy was. We parted. But I learned not to-- not to treat people like that. Now I listen to everybody and I give them their due. What was the next major event that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the next major event was the tent camp was terrible. We got bombed one night by two planes. The bomb fell next to me and Yehuda Backon, right next to us. The crater was right next to us. We sort of looked, and there was the earth. You know, it sort of blew over us, all the dirt. And the-- the person who was sleeping next to me-- we were sleeping in the open-- disappeared with this crater. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And next morning, people were cooking meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they get the meat from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Human meat. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Now it was interesting how the Hungarian Jews, who still had their packages and their families with them in this tent camp, regarded us boys. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They regarded us as people who would steal from them. And suddenly, we weren't children anymore. We were now young tearaways who were dangerous, were a gang of dangerous boys. We weren't. But that's the-- there was a sudden change. It-- up till now I was treated as a kid by grownups. Other prisoners were helping me. And suddenly these prisoners were not helping us at all, and they were cooking meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of anyone yourself that was eating this meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I saw them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were feeding their children with this meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they, they-- well, A, they wouldn't give us, and B, I shied away. I couldn't go to the latrine there. The latrine that was made was a hole in the ground, which a tractor dug out. And they built a sort of tripod all the way around this hole so that you should sit on the tripod over the hole. And the, the-- it was just stakes hammered into the earth. And you know, it was raining, and it was a wet spring time. So people would sit there, dysentery and so forth. I saw somebody fall in, drown in the-- drown in the muck. So I didn't go. I felt like I hadn't been to the toilet for days. I hadn't eaten for days either. So you see-- probably. But I needed to go. You couldn't relieve yourself anywhere except at this pit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Incidentally, again, Mauthausen is on a hill, and the tent camp was halfway up the hill. So you could see this wonderful Austrian valley with woods stretching up the other side, you know. It's lovely countryside. And you'd say to yourself, you know, I'd like to go for a walk in the woods there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there villages nearby that you could see?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Just, just countryside. So then they called that people should go up into the camp on the hill, so I thought, it can't be worse than here. At least there's organization in the camp. So I got into the camp. And there were some other Birkenau boys there as well. And one of them told me that the camp fire brigade is looking for boy servants, you know, to polish their boots and--

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp fire brigades were also German prisoners who were trusted prisoners by the Germans. And their function was like a public fire brigade. They went out with the fire truck to surrounding towns and villages, and they fought fires. They're all Germans. And I sort of said to the guard, I want to go to the fire brigade because I hear they want-- they called it PIPL-- P-I-P-L. To be a PIPL went like, like a batman, you know? So I go up to the fire brigade and I talk to one of these firemen, and he says yes, you can be my PIPL. And he immediately organized the bunk for me and gave me some food. And I was again in this situation where a grown-up was helping me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there were about four or five boys like that in this fire brigade thing. The fire brigade barrack in Mauthausen was right at the main gate of the camp. The main gate of the camp was sort of granite big gate with towers on either side like a castle, granite walls with wire above. So you couldn't see out. Once you were in the camp in Mauthausen, you couldn't see out because of the walls. So again, I had this feeling of, you know, being closed in. And then these other boys, they were all masturbating with each other. And they immediately wanted me to participate. And when I demurred-- oh, I have to tell you, the block next to ours, next to the fire brigade block, was the bordello. There was a camp bordello in Mauthausen which had Jewish women in it, not German women. And the fire brigade, every time they went to fight a fire, they'd get so many tickets. And the number-- the tickets were like camp money, and they could give up so many tickets was a visit to the bordello, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the fellow that I was batman to was going every night to the bordello, and every night they went on duty, you know, to fight a fire somewhere. And he'd bring back all sorts of nice things, things that were saved from the larder in the fire. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So these other batboys wanted to sort of initiate me into this homosexuality. And I said no, no. I'm-- you, you-- I'm not going to. So I remember this fellow saying to me, look-- Brauman, or Berman, or whatever his name was-- he's got tickets for the bordello, but when he run out of tickets he'll want your ass. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] next morning they said, we are evacuating Mauthausen. People can volunteer to go on one of these marches. So I volunteered to save myself from a fate worse than death. And we went on this march to Gunskirchen, which, two or three days, countryside. You went by foot all the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Foot, yes. Not-- it wasn't a-- it's not a big distance. But it's a big distance. We were walking around villages. We were taking the long route. Again, keep out of the-- And I think the second evening, we stopped at a clearing, little woods, again, lovely countryside. And you walk into the wood. Obviously other marchers have been through there. It was full of feces. You know, I thought I'd find berries, but the place was full of shit-- excuse me-- because, you know, other, other marchers had been through this site.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I walk out disconsolately, terribly hungry. And up a little bit of a rise, a farm road sort of going at  a slant up, I see a van stopping. The SS open the back doors and they are starting to hand out whole loaves of bread. Were you still wearing a uniform with the paint across it at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I start fighting my way up, but it was all grown-ups, you know, so you can't-- I give up. And then I think to myself, a whole loaf of bread, you know? And you could smell the bread. It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I fought my way through this melange. And the SS decided, you know, according to standing orders, when there is a riot, we start beating. And we're going to lock the van and we're not going to hand out any more bread. And that was just when I got there. And they started beating everybody over the head. And I had to shove, you know, not to get asphyxiated because it's like a football crowd, it's really-- there was a crush. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I get a-- one of these sticks lands right on my cranium. But luckily I had fought my way to the side of this rise, and I fall down the embankment, sort of roll down, and I pass out. This was the second time I passed out. And I sort of come to, and I see the van is sort of all clear. And they are driving the bread off. And I still see people walking around, you know, with bread, which they got out of the van. So I could have been out for minutes, perhaps, not more than a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I touch myself, you know. I'm all there, but I've got a big bump on my head. And it hurts, very tender. And I stagger about. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They did-- by this time the Germans didn't even bother to count us. There's no appell. Suddenly in the dusk, I hear in German the prisoners calling, [GERMAN]? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I run towards where they are calling, and I come to some Polish prisoners. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I knew it couldn't be Leo because Leo is in Germany somewhere in Saxony. So, you know, and I am south of the Danube in Austria. I knew the difference between Germany and Austria. It seemed inconceivable to me that it was Leo, but his name is being called. And Leo Honigwachs comes up and it's a stranger. So they're all getting ready to run away. So I say to this Leo Honigwachs, where are you from, you know. Who are you? My name is Honigwachs. And we always knew that Honigwachs is such a rare name we have to be related. And he named some Polish city, which is meaningless to me. And now I know the geography very well because I've done some studies because of this genealogy work that I do. But even now people say to me names of shtetls which I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he refuses to take me. He says, you can't run and you'll, you'll hold us up. We're not taking you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's like a few weeks before the end of the war. I've never discovered this Leo Honigwachs, although I have found another Leo Honigwachs in Canada who is my fifth cousin. It's another story about genealogy.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we get to this camp in, in Gunskirchen, which is just wooden huts in the middle of a forest. And there's no fence, and we're just dumped there. And now and then they give us soup, and we're terribly overcrowded. The overcrowding was the worst thing, and the-- by this time the hunger and the thirst was the worst. Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the, the, the roof trusses were made of timber. And there were two timbers with another timber running in between. So during the night I shimmied up into this roof truss and I slept on the roof truss. So I had room to stretch out. But on the other hand, I couldn't sleep because I was afraid of falling down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you sort of sleep a half sleep. You're stretched out and you're resting. But I've often watched monkeys now, and I wonder how they are assured in the trees. Because I felt all this time, any moment I was going to fall. I mean, my fall would have been soft because I would have fallen on people's heads. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we started getting lice, everyone. We were living in such close proximity to each other. And Yehuda Backon was there, and Misha Krause was there, various other boys. We would take our shirts off and our clothes off in the morning. We'd have a louse hunt, and we'd kill the lice in between our thumbnails, and they make a little pop sound as you crunch them. And some people were eating them because the blood, you know, you-- I mean, we didn't know about typhus and all this business. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the thirst was worse than the hunger. So one night I say to myself, we're going to die here. Now is the time. And I crawl through this forest, and I take any direction because we're in a forest. You can't see where we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop here.
